codingStandards.paradigm: Clean, readable code with clear separation of concerns
rules: No magic numbers or hardcoded secrets
Error handling must be explicit — no silent catches
Use meaningful variable and function names

JavaScript: Use const by default, let only when needed, never var
Use strict equality (===) instead of loose equality (==)
TypeScript: All functions should have explicit return types
Python: Use type hints for function parameters and return types
React: Use functional components with hooks instead of class components
Avoid inline styles — use CSS modules, styled-components, or Tailwind
Memoize expensive computations with useMemo and callbacks with useCallback
Keep components small and focused — extract reusable logic into custom hooks
Next.js: Use Server Components by default, Client Components only when needed (useState, useEffect, event handlers)
Use next/image for optimized image loading and next/link for client-side navigation
Fetch data in Server Components or use Route Handlers — avoid client-side fetching for initial data
Never expose server-only secrets or API keys in Client Components
HTML: Use semantic HTML elements (header, nav, main, section, footer)
Always include alt attributes on images for accessibility
CSS: Use CSS variables for repeated values like colors and spacing
Node.js: Never use synchronous file or network operations in request handlers
Always validate and sanitize user input before processing
Java: Follow Java naming conventions (camelCase for methods, PascalCase for classes)
C#: Follow .NET naming conventions (PascalCase for public members)
Go: Always check returned errors — never discard with _
Rust: Prefer Result/Option over unwrap() in production code
Use clippy recommendations for idiomatic Rust
SQL: Always use parameterized queries — never concatenate user input
Avoid SELECT * — specify only the columns needed
Shell: Always quote variables to prevent word splitting
Use set -euo pipefail at the top of scripts
